front: What is the cardinality of a set?
back: The cardinality of a set is a measure of the 'number of elements' in the set. For a finite set, it is simply the count of its distinct elements. For infinite sets, cardinality is a more general concept used to compare sizes.

front: Define what it means for two sets to have the same cardinality.
back: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if there exists a bijective function (a one-to-one correspondence) from A to B. This is denoted as |A| = |B|.
A set is countable if it is finite or if it has the same cardinality as the set of natural numbers ℕ (i.e., |A| = |ℕ|). An infinite countable set is also called countably infinite or denumerable.

front: What is the cardinality of the set of real numbers, and what is it called?
back: The cardinality of the set of real numbers ℝ is called the cardinality of the continuum. It is strictly greater than the cardinality of the natural numbers (|ℝ| > |ℕ|) and is denoted by 𝖈 (c) or 2^(ℵ₀).

The Schröder-Bernstein Theorem states that if there exist injective functions f: A → B and g: B → A, then there exists a bijective function h: A → B. This means |A| = |B| (the sets have the same cardinality).
